sentences.text: Good morning.
name is Morton Fuchs.
F-U-C-H-S.
My name was Miksa, M-I-K-S-A.
No.
No, I don't have.
My birthday is in June 30, 1911.
Well, 85 and a half.
In June I'm going to be 86.
I was born in Tokaj, a little town in the Hungary.
It's 250 kilometer from Budapest to east.
T-O-K-A-J.
Well, in my family, I'm the only one who survived.
Well, my brother, well, he was born in 1901.
And with wife and two children, he was deported and he didn't survive, none of them.
My father was Hinrik.
He was Hungarian.
H-I-N-R-I-K.
Well, with the whole family it was kind of warm family.
We had a good relationship with our parents, with my brother and sister.
We had a good, warm relationship.
Well, he had a general store for 47 years, and the whole family worked in the store.
And after 47 years, he retired.
And my brother and myself, we will continue during the Holocaust.
Well, he was a very religious man.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he was very friendly to everybody.
And he tried to help everybody.
Many people came to him for advice or for help.
And he was available to everybody.
He was a very good man.
Brought ?] [? respect ?] all over in the city.
And my mother was Ida, I-D-A.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Last name?
Last name was [INAUDIBLE].
She was a daughter of a rabbi.
Well, she was a fine person, nice person.
And friendly all the time, smiled all the time.
And helped the children and everybody.
So it's a very, very friendly good person, my mother.
We were four, two girl and two boy.
My older sister was Ilona.
Then my brother, Vilmos-- William.
My younger sister was Miriam.
And myself, Miksa.
We had a good relation.
Sometimes we were fighting, just like anywhere, children fighting with each other.
But I think we loved each other.
It was a good relationship.
Well, home life, it was a strictly kosher home, a very religious family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father and my brother and myself, we went to the temple twice a day, the morning and in the evening.
My mother, they-- the girls didn't go to the temple, just maybe twice, three times a year.
My mother on special occasions went to the temple.
But at home, my sisters, my mother, all had the daily prayers.
We observed all the holidays very strictly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a happy home.
Favorite what?
Holiday.
Pesach, Seder night.
I wished, I always wished my father had made a Seder so beautifully.
We had all the family together and some guests.
And I wished it would be for a week every evening where we would [INAUDIBLE] That was my favorite holiday.
The Seder?
Well, I was the youngest in the family.
And I was [INAUDIBLE] And I was always wondering, why have this question, I already know the answer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But it was nice.
The Seder?
Oh, Shabbat.
So we went to the temple, of course.
We came back.
It was a very [INAUDIBLE] We had dinner.
It wasn't lunch over there.
We call it dinner.
And in the afternoon [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] friends, they come together.
And four or five o'clock there was another service in the temple, and we went to the temple again.
And the temple was a social-- it was not just for praying, but was a social gathering, where we meet friends and discuss things happening.
So it was a nice life.
Religious?
Yes.
It was customary in Europe, or in Hungary, I'm sure in other Eastern European cities, boy start the age of four or five, went to the cheder.
The cheder was a Hebrew school.
And there we were learning to eat Hebrew and speak Yiddish.
I learned to speak Yiddish.
And right around we were studying the five books of Moses.
An then always we had-- year after year, we [INAUDIBLE] education [INAUDIBLE].
Jewish life.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this little town where I come from, most of the people were cultural Jews.
And they would observe the Sabbath and holidays.
There was people who didn't have a kosher home, didn't observe the Sabbath.
But there was still a religious life.
There was a [INAUDIBLE].
There was a, you know, the [INAUDIBLE].
Make sure they have a [INAUDIBLE] in the house.
And then there was other organization what was really nice.
They were called [INAUDIBLE].
It was the taking care of the poor of the city.
Because over there it wasn't any social security or pension.
People who get old and was unable to work, so they didn't have any income, so they stayed with their children.
Some people didn't have children, or the children they lived far away, so they lived by themself.
They didn't have any income, so this organization helped them.
I can remember it was every week before Sabbath, or the two weeks on rotation.
But this organization send out care packages for these people.
In the evening, somebody that-- I delivered many times, and it set the package at the door step.
Nobody knew where they come from, and nobody know where they got it.
So those people, they were not embarrassed to get charity.
And there was many way to support the poor people.
Like an example, there was a retired [INAUDIBLE] over there.
Didn't have any income.
One [INAUDIBLE] he prepared roses for the Seder night.
And people went over there and picked up little roses and gave him some money.
That was one income.
Or there was other family I know, they were renting a room.
There was one room and a kitchen.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] In Europe, in Hungary, was old people, they went to town and the asked for charity.
Went to door to door asked for charity.
Now, those people stay over there at night, so they have to sleep somewhere.
Those people, they rented a room.
They were sleeping in the kitchen, rented a room.
And the congregation paid for them, for those strange people who were sleeping there.
There was many, many little things but they help each other people.
It was really a nice congregation.
Everybody knows [INAUDIBLE] when there was a [INAUDIBLE] or a bris or something, everybody was there.
There was 1,200.
Older people, younger people.
And survived maybe 100, 120.
Well, the population was 9,000, almost 10,000.
It was a real town.
It was good relationship, yeah.
It really wasn't anti-Semitism.
But after the-- before the Second War and during the second one, there was anti-Semitism.
But after the First War in the '20s, I remember '20s, '30s, it wasn't an anti.
Was very-- their relationship was very good with the Christian people.
Friends.
We had many friends.
But there was a very special friend, Mr.
Farkas.
I remember Friday evening after dinner they came over.
Mr.
Farkas was talking with my father and Mrs.
Farkas was talking with my mother.
And we children were sitting around and were listening to them.
And I remember Mr.
Farkas was always so nervous, because he smoked a pipe.
And Sabbath he couldn't smoke a pipe because he was a religious man.
He was so nervous he could hardly wait to over Sabbath so he could smoke his pipe.
And there was-- my aunt was there.
My mother's sister lived next door to us.
And came over, or we went over there.
It was a nice family relationship.
No, no.
We didn't celebrate-- the children-- well, they knew-- my mother knew it's your birthday, then made some favor-- buy your favorite cake for my sister, my brother.
My favorite was the cheese pie.
And I remember, when I had a birthday my mother made cheese pie.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.
But there wasn't any birthday celebration.
There wasn't any birthday card.
There wasn't even bar mitzvah card.
No, no, there wasn't things like that.
When a boy was bar mitzvah-- there wasn't girl bar mitzvah, not such a thing.
A boy for his bar mitzvah, he didn't get presents.
He would get [INAUDIBLE] from his close relatives, but they can send it out invitations.
But the [INAUDIBLE] in that time, or Mr.
And Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE] congregation for the bar mitzvah party.
And people came.
It was like an open house.
People came and gone.
And this went on till a couple hours.
But everybody was invited and everybody came.
Yeah, I had a bar mitzvah also.
Yeah.
How was my bar mitzvah?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I tell you, so the Hebrew teacher prepared the bar mitzvah boy for the bar mitzvah.
And also he wrote a little speech for the bar mitzvah boy to say this speech during the party.
My father was very strict.
And he said, a bar mitzvah boy has to be able to make up his speech.
He said, you read the five books of Moses and every word there is a message.
And you have to make up your speech.
If not, you will get it.
I don't want [INAUDIBLE] to make up your speech.
So I did beautiful at the temple.
They have Torah.
And came to open house.
People came and they look at me, you know, where is the speech?
They're asking about-- they look at him.
They were expecting I was going to say something.
And I couldn't.
I wasn't so smart.
I couldn't make up.
There was a good friend of my father, Mr.
Greenwald, was a good friend of my father.
And he saw the situation.
And he said in the Yiddish, tell me, what was you learned last week in the cheder about the [YIDDISH] and the [YIDDISH]?
Was the world built to be for the [YIDDISH] or the [YIDDISH]?
So I remember I was learned about that.
And I thought and gave right it right away, a little speech, about few minutes.
So I was so happy.
And people came and gone.
And they look at me.
So I start to talk again about the [YIDDISH].
So that's what I remember.
And I remember my mother and my two sisters, they were baking for days.
It wasn't officially [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] So anyway, there was so much cookies.
They didn't serve a dig dinner like here.
There were cookies and some wine, some biscuits, that's it.
There wasn't Gefilte fish or nothing, that's it.
And people were helping themselves.
So that's what a bar mitzvah looked like in those times.
Well, I had a four elementary school and four-year high school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, of course, in the Hebrew school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
After the bar mitzvah, usually the higher education was to go to [INAUDIBLE] yeshiva.
But my father needed me in the store.
So it wasn't-- there was a yeshiva in Tokaj.
But that time we didn't have a yeshiva because the rabbi passed away a year before and we didn't have a new rabbi.
What we we had are dayan.
You know what a dayan is?
Rabbi-- actually, dayan means a judge.
And he was also a rabbi.
Not assistant, but a second rabbi.
And over there I went and studied privately maybe a year or two year.
And I didn't-- I really didn't like it.
Somehow I didn't like it, so I quit about two years.
And then I was working full in the store.
It was a Jewish school, yeah.
So everybody religious had the separate elementary school.
The Jewish congregation had their own school.
The Catholic congregation had their own school.
And there were the [INAUDIBLE] and Methodists.
And there were several schools, the elementary school.
Then the high school was public.
That was public.
But my father-- we were very religious, so my mother didn't let me go to the public school because we have to go over there on Shabbos.
So that's why I took the high school private.
There were several Jewish kids who were privately.
Hungarian.
In the cheder, in the Hebrew school, Yiddish.
It wasn't allowed to speak other language, only Yiddish.
And that's how I learned.
My parents, together, most of the time, they spoke German.
Because my father came from a district of Hungary where people mostly were speaking German, and also my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Most of the time they were sure speaking German.
Well, we had the daily paper, and several magazines.
My sister [INAUDIBLE] separate magazine.
My brother had and I had a magazine.
And that's all.
I tell you, we didn't have much time to read, because early in the morning till late night, we all, the whole family, we were working in the store.
Shabbos we come together with friends and we had fun.
So we didn't have reading much time.
Yeah.
Well, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't see any magazines like that here.
It was many [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
And the funny pages of the day.
The comic section was there.
And little stories, little stories from-- every day stories.
It was very-- I remember I liked very much.
It was very interesting.
[NON-ENGLISH], that was the name.
I don't know.
I don't really know.
Well, I think I was--
